coredata.dc:title: Plasma nitriding effect on tribological and corrosion properties of Stellite 6 and 12 PTA weld clad hardfaced on stainless steel 410
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Poshtahani, Alireza Gholami
Roostaie, Saied
Azadi, Mahboobeh
coredata.dc:description: Cladded Stellites on steel substrates are utilized vastly in harsh environments. Since the plasma nitriding process can raise the tribological behavior of the material surface. Thus, the main purpose of this paper is to investigate tribological and corrosion characteristics of nitrided Stellite 6 and 12 that were cladded through a plasma-transferred arc (PTA) method on stainless steel 410. A Pin-on-disk tribometer, Tafel polarization, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy were utilized to study the properties of various coatings. The X-ray diffraction patterns indicated that both cladded coatings mainly included solid solution phase ( γ -Co) and chromium-rich carbide phases. However, after the plasma nitriding (PN) process, nitrided phases such as CrN, CoN, and W2N were found at the surface of coatings. The PN process caused a reduction in wear rates for both Stellite coatings by about 89–98%. This reduction was due to the hardness increase and the friction coefficient decrease. However, this PN process increased the corrosion rate of Stellites in a 3.5% wt NaCl solution. Such behavior was based on forming brittle and hard nitrided phases that promote surface cracks and open the path for ion corrosion penetration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Plasma nitriding
Stellite
Corrosion
Microstructure
Wear
Plasma-Transferred Arc Welding

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: A suggested mechanism for corrosion reactions

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Top view of specimens before the PN process (a) S6, (b) S12, and (c) S12 after the polishing process.
OM images from a cross-section view for nitrided specimens included (a) NS6, and (b) NS12.
SEM images of a cross-section view of two nitrided specimens included (a) NS6 in SE mode, (b) NS6 in BSE mode, and (c) NS12.
Micro-hardness profile versus the distance from the edge of specimens.
COF plots versus wear distance for all specimens included (a) S6, (b) S12, (c) NS6, and (d) NS12.
Worn surfaces of all specimens included, (a) S6, (b) S12, (c) NS6, and (d) NS12.
EDS results from the worn surface of the NS6 specimen.
(a) Plots of OCP versus the immersion time, (b) Tafel polarization plots for all specimens in a 3.5% wt NaCl solution.
(a) Nyquist and (b) phase angle plots for various specimens, (c) a scheme for a suggested electrical circuit to fit EIS measurements.
Suggested schemes for corrosion reactions, (a) for non-nitrided specimens and (b) for nitrided specimens.
Details of the composition of stainless steel 410 as substrate (% wt).
Parameters of the PTA coating process.
Mean surface hardness, the mean coefficient of friction (COF), and the specific wear rate of all specimens.
Tafel polarization measurements extracted from Fig. 9(b).
EIS measurements extracted data from Fig. 10.

head.ce:title: Plasma nitriding effect on tribological and corrosion properties of Stellite 6 and 12 PTA weld clad hardfaced on stainless steel 410

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Metallurgical Engineering, Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST), Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Metallurgical Engineering, Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Metallurgical Engineering, Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST), Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials and Metallurgical Engineering, Semnan University, Semnan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials and Metallurgical Engineering, Semnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials and Metallurgical Engineering, Semnan University, Semnan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Mavad Fannavar Mehr Company, Karaj, Alborz Province, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Mavad Fannavar Mehr Company, Karaj, Alborz Province, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cladded Stellites on steel substrates are utilized vastly in harsh environments. Since the plasma nitriding process can raise the tribological behavior of the material surface. Thus, the main purpose of this paper is to investigate tribological and corrosion characteristics of nitrided Stellite 6 and 12 that were cladded through a plasma-transferred arc (PTA) method on stainless steel 410. A Pin-on-disk tribometer, Tafel polarization, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy were utilized to study the properties of various coatings. The X-ray diffraction patterns indicated that both cladded coatings mainly included solid solution phase ( γ -Co) and chromium-rich carbide phases. However, after the plasma nitriding (PN) process, nitrided phases such as CrN, CoN, and W2N were found at the surface of coatings. The PN process caused a reduction in wear rates for both Stellite coatings by about 89–98%. This reduction was due to the hardness increase and the friction coefficient decrease. However, this PN process increased the corrosion rate of Stellites in a 3.5% wt NaCl solution. Such behavior was based on forming brittle and hard nitrided phases that promote surface cracks and open the path for ion corrosion penetration.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Stellite alloys are a set of cobalt-based alloys which are widely utilized in harsh environments based on their proper resistance to abrasion, wear, and corrosion (Zhu et al., 2017; Smolina and Kobiela, 2021; Chen et al., 2008). Moreover, they can be used as hard-facing coatings through cladding and welding methods on soft materials such as carbon steel or stainless steel (Chen et al., 2008; Motallebzadeh et al., 2015). In this situation, they are utilized in drastic applications such as gas turbines, aerospace, petrochemical, agricultural tools, and mining industries since they enhance surface properties (Motallebzadeh et al., 2015; Bartkowski and Kinal, 2017; Kim et al., 1995). In recent years, the effort of researchers was to investigate various ways to enhance the properties of Stellite 6 and 12 as cobalt-based alloys, as follows,

Zhu et al. (2017) studied the wear properties of Stellite 6 alloy that was hardened through plasma arc processes. They showed that the wear rate at room temperature was lower than 300 °C. Smolina and Kobiela (2021) studied the corrosion and wear resistance of Stellite 6 laser-surfaced alloyed with rhenium. Chen et al. (2008) enhanced the wear resistance of Stellite 21 alloy through a plasma surface alloying method. Their results displayed that the wear rate of Stellite 21 in 3.5% NaCl solution and air decreased by about 95% through utilized surface treatment. Motallebzadeh et al. (2015) evaluated the microstructure and wear properties of Stellite 12 coatings on steel substrates. They found that the Stellite12 hard-facing coating had higher wear resistance than steel substrates. Brownlie et al. (2017) investigated the effect of the nitriding process on the wear performance of a single and double-layer Stellite 6 through a hot wire tungsten inert gas (HWTIG) method. Their results demonstrated that the nitriding process resulted in a high surface hardness of about 400–680 HV. Thiagarajani and Ponnusamy (2021) investigated the wear properties of Stellite 6 cladding on the stainless steel 316L alloy through the cold metal arc transfer process. Their result showed that the welding current was an effective parameter for the microhardness changes. Bartkowski and Kinal (2016) evaluated the wear rates of Stellite 6/WC coatings fabricated by laser cladding. They demonstrated that the WC particles in the Stellite 6 matrix caused a micro-cutting mechanism in wear testing. Xiong et al. (2019) utilized a tungsten inert gas cladding method to deposit Stellite 6 alloy coating onto the F91 steel. They found that formed brittle phases were the cause of cracks during failure. Consequently, various researchers used types of Stellites as coatings on steels, and the wear characteristics of these coatings were investigated. However, the evaluation of wear and the corrosion resistance of these coatings simultaneously with the effect of plasma nitriding has been performed in limited studies.

Therefore, as a new study, the aim of this paper is to evaluate the effect of plasma nitriding (PN) on wear resistance and corrosion properties of cladded Stellite 6 and 12 on the stainless steel 410 through a PTA method. It was notable that the plasma-transferred arc (PTA) was used as a deposition method. This method was a proper and cost-effective process for metallic coatings, based on the high deposition rate, good adhesion, and excellent process stability (Baiamonte et al., 2017). This method can be also used as a repairing technique to increase the service life of various components (Mandal et al., 2015). Notably, the application of this steel is in pumps, shafts, gas turbine blades, valve parts, nozzles, bolts, nuts, and screws. Since these components can be exposed to wear conditions in marine applications. Therefore, pin-on-disk tribometer, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Tafel polarization were performed to study the tribological and corrosion characteristics of various coatings in a 3.5% wt NaCl solution.

The utilized substrates were stainless steel 410 (SS 410). They were rebars with a diameter of 10 cm. The detail of chemical composition was obtained through a spark emission spectrometer (Bruker model) and is reported in Table 1.

The powder of Stellite 6 contained 28.5 Cr, 4.4 W, 1.1 C wt % balanced with Co. Moreover, the powder of Stellite 12 included 28.5 Cr, 8.0 W, 1.4 C wt% balanced with Co. However, the content of other elements such as Ni, Fe, Mo, Si, and Mn was lower than 0.1 wt%. Powders were purchased from Sentesbir Company. The size of both powders was in a range of 53 to 150 μ m.

To facilitate the transfer of heat during the cladding process, at first, steel substrates were welded on a copper sheet and preheated at a temperature of 150 °C. Then, in the next stage, the coating process through a plasma-transferred arc welding (PTA) method was performed. Thus, Stellite 6 and 12 were coated on steel rebar in the size of 8 × 8 cm2 (Fig. 1(a) and (b)). The rebar was put in a 200 °C furnace for a slow cooling rate. Two coated specimens were called S6 and S12.
The parameters of the coating process are noted in Table 2. After the end of the process, the copper sheet was separated from the steel substrate.

Before the PN process, the surface of cladded specimens was ground and polished, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The thickness of the coatings was reduced to 3000 μ m after the grinding process. The temperature and time of the nitriding process were 560 °C and 14 h, respectively. Other PN parameters was also shown in Table 3. More details of the PN process were also found in the other research (Aghareb Parast et al., 2021; Aghajani and Behrangi, 2017; Yazdani et al., 2014). Two nitrided specimens were called NS6 and NS12.

At first, the surface of specimens was ground with SiC papers up to 2000 girt and polished with 0.25 μ m alumina paste. Then, they were etched in a solution with Marble’s etchant. The etchant contained HCl 37% (50 ml), CuSO4 (10 g), and H2O (50 ml) (Azadi et al., 2017). Microstructure evaluations were performed by optical microscopy (OM) and scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with an energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS). The phase detection was examined through an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu-k α radiation.

The wear properties of specimens were evaluated in a dry condition through a ball-on-disk tribometer. The test temperature was about 35 °C. The ball was made of zirconia with a diameter of 7 mm. The applied normal force was about 7.6 N. The total sliding distance in wear testing was 500 m. The sliding velocity was 0.1 m/s. The specific wear rate of coatings (K) can be calculated through the following relation K = Δ W/
F × S
, where
Δ W
is weight loss, F and S are applied force and distance during wear testing, respectively (Azadi et al., 2014; Wang et al., 2010). In addition, the worn surfaces of specimens were examined by an SEM to study the wear mechanism.
A Vickers micro-hardness indenter was utilized on the surface of specimens to determine specimens’ hardness. The applied load was 100 g.
For hardness and wear tests, three specimens were utilized and a mean value of measurements was reported to show the repeatability of the work.

Electrochemical tests were performed using a potentiostat (Autolab-OGF500) through a flat cell and three electrodes. A saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was utilized as a reference electrode. The auxiliary electrode was a platinum sheet. Specimens with an area of 10 mm2 were working electrodes. The corrosive medium was a 3.5% wt NaCl solution. A similar environment was also utilized in other research (Chen et al., 2008; Ma et al., 2018) for Stellites since this solution is known as a simulated seawater solution for marine applications. Tests were done at a temperature of 35 °C. The open-circuit potential (OCP) was monitored for 1800 s when specimens were immersed in the test solution. In the polarization test, the rate of applied potential change was 0.5 mV/s. The applied potential in Tafel polarization and EIS tests was ± 250 mV and ± 10 mV around the OCP, respectively. The frequency range in the EIS tests was 105 to 10−2 Hz. The exposure time for specimens in both corrosion tests was about 30 mins.

Fig. 2 shows OM images from a cross-section view for nitrided specimens. The dominant phase for coatings was the Co phase as a matrix and seen in a dendritic structure. Moreover, the carbide phase was observed in black-colored areas. A similar dendritic structure for Stellite 6 was also found in other research (Smolina and Kobiela, 2021; Thiagarajani and Ponnusamy, 2021; Díaz et al., 2012; Kiehl et al., 2022). This structure was based on the planar solidification during the cladding process. Notably, the length of dendritic branches for Stellite 12 was lower than for Stellite 6. Such an observation was also reported in another investigation (Díaz et al., 2012). The nitrided layer thickness for both specimens was about 20 μ m. As shown in Fig. 2(b), a diffusion zone formed between the cladded layer and SS 410 as substrate. However, based on the over-etching, the substrate was observed in a black-colored area. It seemed that there were no defects in the interface of SS 410/Stellite 12. Moreover, the heat-affected zone (HAZ) was also found under the diffusion zone with a length of about 100 μ m.

Fig. 3 displays the cross-sectional SEM micrograph of specimens after the PN process without any etching process. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the thickness of the white-colored layer in both coatings was about 20 ± 5 μ m. Most of the nitrided phases were precipitated in this region. Fig. 3(b) shows the presence of carbides for specimen NS6 in the back-scattered electron mode (BSE). Based on the cooling rate of the coating process, a columnar cobalt-rich solid solution matrix would be appeared for specimens, as shown in Fig. 3(c). A similar structure was also reported in other research (Motallebzadeh et al., 2015). The presence of nitrided precipitates (seen in black-colored areas) was dominant for specimen NS12.

Fig. 4 shows XRD patterns for all specimens. X-ray patterns revealed that the cobalt solid-solution ( γ -Co) (JCPDS No. 00-15-0806) was a dominant phase for all specimens. Three peaks in planes (111), (200), and (220) were related to this phase. It was found that some amount of Cr and W could be solved in the Co phase as a soft matrix (Pfohl and Rie, 2001; Chen et al., 2019). Specimens also included carbides with a chemical composition of (Cr, Fe)7C3. A similar XRD pattern for Stellite 6 was found in other research (Zhu et al., 2017; Brownlie et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2019). However, another carbide phase of Cr23C6 was also found in Stellite 12. Similar XRD results for Stellite 12 were also reported in another study (Motallebzadeh et al., 2015). It was reported that chromium as the main alloying element in Stellite would be reacted with carbon to form carbides (Zhu et al., 2017). This reaction resulted in the formation of two types of carbides
M
7
C3 and
M
23
C6 (Bartkowski et al., 2015). It was notable that the carbon content was responsible for the formed carbide type (Bartkowski et al., 2015). By applying the PN process, nitrided phases such as CoN and CrN would be formed for both nitrided Stellites. The peak intensity for the CrN phase was higher than the CoN phase. Three peaks in planes (111), (220), and (222) were related to the CrN phase. Similar peaks for nitrided Stellite 21 were also found in other research (Chen et al., 2008; Kim et al., 1995). However, another phase of W2N was also exhibited for Stellite 12. The formation of such a phase could be attributed to the higher amount of tungsten in Stellite 12 compared to Stellite 6.

Table 4 reports the measurement of mean micro-hardness for the surface of all specimens. The PN process increased the surface hardness of both coatings. The surface hardness for the nitrided surface of specimen NS12 increased from about 566 to 1001 HV, representing an increase of 1.75 times. However, such an increase in the hardness of specimen NS6 was about 1.26 times in comparison with the substrate. A high microhardness for nitrided surfaces was the result of the formation of nitrided phases such as CrN, W2N, and CoN. However, the hardness of Stellite in comparison with the stainless steel 410 was related to the formation of complex carbides of (Cr, Fe)
7
C
3
and Cr
23
C
6
types (Kim et al., 1995; Azadi et al., 2018a). A similar hardness (about 300–400 VHN) for Stellite 6 (Smolina and Kobiela, 2021; Bartkowski and Kinal, 2016; Xiong et al., 2019) and 500 VHN for Stellite 12 (Baiamonte et al., 2017) was also reported by other studies. Based on the higher content of C and W in Stellite 12 compared to Stellite 6, the higher hardness of Stellite 12 was predictable as found in other research (Díaz et al., 2012). Moreover, the fine dendritic structure for Stellite 12 was also a factor to enhance the hardness value.

In addition, Fig. 5 shows the micro-hardness profile versus the distance from the top edge of specimens. The diffusion layer in the nitriding process for both nitrided specimens was about 500 μ m. In this region, the nitrogen diffused into the cobalt structure. Lower nitrided precipitates would be formed and lower hardness would be achieved compared to the nitrided layer. Moreover, the welding zone length was about 1000 μ m. The hardness of SS 410 as substrate was also about 250 VHN.

It was found that in the nitriding process, two regions would be usually formed at the surface of materials: a white layer and a diffusion region. Most nitrided phases were precipitated in the white layer with higher hardness. In this situation, the increase in the hardness would be justified through the precipitation mechanism. However, in the diffusion region, the nitrogen would be diffused fast in the cobalt matrix and the increase in the hardness was due to the solid-solution mechanism (Chen et al., 2008; Aghareb Parast et al., 2021).

Fig. 6 displays the variation of coefficient of friction (COF) during the wear testing under dry conditions for all specimens. In addition, the mean value of COF over the wear distance is reported in Table 4. Plots in Fig. 6 indicated that for all specimens the friction coefficient initially increased rapidly and then, reached a stable state with the increase in the wear distance. However, there were fluctuations during the stable state region. For Stellite12 and 6, the region of the stable state happened before 100 m. However, for the nitrided coatings, this distance increased to 200 m. This could be attributed to the higher hardness of nitrided surfaces. A similar trend in changing COF behavior was also found in other research (Zhu et al., 2017; Baiamonte et al., 2017). The range of COF values for all specimens was about 0.6–0.7. However, the mean value of COF for nitrided specimens changed insignificantly. It was notable that Kim et al. (1995) found that the PN process increased the COF values of Stellite 6. In addition, it was reported that the mean value of COF was about 0.7 for Stellite 6 (Zhu et al., 2017) and 0.8 for Stellite 12 (Motallebzadeh et al., 2015; Baiamonte et al., 2017).

The specific wear rates for all specimens are determined and reported in Table 4. The highest wear rate was attributed to specimen S6 with a lower hardness. The lowest wear rate was related to nitrided surfaces. This event was based on the higher hardness of these specimens. Moreover, it was found that the enhancement in the wear resistance of nitrided Stellite would be attributed to the CrN precipitates as a hard phase (Wang et al., 2010). The wear rate value of specimens S6 and S12 was low compared to steel substrates. It was found that the wear resistance of Stellite alloys was associated with higher hardness based on the presence of carbide phases (Motallebzadeh et al., 2015; Azadi et al., 2014). The wear rates of nitrided surfaces decreased by about 89.7–98.2%.

For a better discussion of wear rate, the ratio of COF/H was measured for all specimens and is reported in Table 4. It was found that this ratio was an effective parameter to predict the wear behavior of utilized materials. Therefore, a higher ratio of COF/H would have resulted in a higher wear rate of materials. A similar parameter was also introduced in other research (Zhu et al., 2017; Azadi et al., 2020b; Gholipour et al., 2011). However, it was found that other mechanical properties such as elastic modulus and toughness were also effective parameters for THE wear characteristics of materials (Azadi et al., 2018b).

Fig. 7 shows SEM images of worn surfaces. For all specimens, micro-abrasion and ridging were found on the worn surface. However, the parallel abrasion marks in the wear track were much finer for nitrided specimens as compared with other samples. Furthermore, micro-plow scars have also appeared on the surface of nitrided surfaces. This resulted in creating many grooves in the direction of wear sliding. Such an event could confirm the abrasive wear mechanism at room temperature in dry conditions. In addition, accumulated plastic deformation that originated from cyclic sliding action was found on the worn surfaces. Similar behavior was also reported in other investigations (Motallebzadeh et al., 2015; Brownlie et al., 2017). Unhooking the nitrided phase from the matrix of Stellite resulted in a micro-cutting phenomenon. The presence of micro-cutting on the worn surfaces of Stellite 6 was also found by other research (Bartkowski and Kinal, 2017, 2016; Wang et al., 2010). When the surface hardness increased the depth of cutting was reduced. For the nitrided surfaces, the hard nitrided precipitates were crushed and influenced the morphology of worn surfaces of coatings. Traces caused by the chipping of these precipitates were visible as dark regions. By increasing the hardness to the highest value for specimen NS12, a fracture event for the worn layer was also found on the surface. Furthermore, this event could result in the highest value of COF. The presence of debris for nitrided surfaces was more obvious compared to other specimens. It was found that the heterogeneity in the wear track depended on grain orientation (Oliveira et al., 2021). Consequently, it seemed that the wear mechanism changed by the hardness of materials from micro-cutting mode to the state of micro-plow and fracture when the surface hardness increased.

For a better discussion of the wear mechanism, EDS results from the worn surface of specimen NS6 are displayed in Fig. 8. The presence of Zr and O elements on the worn surface showed that the zirconia ball cut the matrix of the coatings. Moreover, ball debris has also remained on the worn surface. The ball debris could be created through the hard nitrided precipitates in the matrix. Furthermore, the presence of carbide precipitates caused fluctuations in COF values during wear testing (Zhu et al., 2017; Gholipour et al., 2011; Ratia et al., 2019). In addition, Ratia et al. (2019) reported that the easily sheared cobalt matrix was the most effective phase during wear testing.

Fig. 9(a) shows the open circuit potential (OCP) versus the immersion time. After 30 min, the potential of all specimens reached a steady state region. However, for nitrided specimens, the OCP values were reduced by increasing the immersion time. This could be attributed to the non-passivation behavior of these specimens. Although for specimens S6 and S12, an insignificant increase in OCP value versus the time was observed. Since changes in OCP values were not high, the formation of the passive layer in a continuous shape was not possible.

Fig. 9(b) displays the graph of current density versus the potential for all specimens. In addition, the extracted data contains corrosion current density (
I
corr
), corrosion potential (
E
corr
), and anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes (
β
a
and
β
c
) are reported in Table 5. The shapes of all plots were similar to each other. There was no passivation region for all specimens in the range of applied potentials. A similar Tafel polarization for Stellite 6 was also found in other research (Bartkowski et al., 2015, 2018). As shown in Table 5, the nitriding process caused an increase in the corrosion current density of both nitrided coatings. Similar behavior was also reported in another paper (Brownlie et al., 2017). Thus, the lowest corrosion rate was related to specimen S12. Since specimen S12 included more content of W to form tungstate oxide as a protective layer, it showed a lower value of
I
corr
compared to specimen S6. Moreover, the nitriding process resulted in more negative corrosion potential. This showed that the thermodynamic tendency of nitrided surfaces to corrosion reactions was higher than the non-nitrided specimens. The value of
E
corr
for all specimens was from −520 to −227 mV. Both anodic and cathodic Tafel slopes increased when specimens were exposed to the nitriding process. This event demonstrated that both anodic and cathodic reactions were affected by the plasma nitriding process. Anodic reactions were metal dissolution containing the dissolution of cobalt (Co=Co
2 +
+ 2e
−
) and chromium (Cr=Cr3＋ + 3e
−
). The cathodic reaction was oxygen reduction (
O
2
＋2
H
2
O + 4e
−
= 4
OH
−
). It was found that for nitrided surfaces, a large content of chromium would be consumed to form the CrN phase. This phenomenon resulted in a reduction in the corrosion resistance of nitrided specimens. It was notable that the presence of chromium element freely in the matrix of non-nitrided specimens caused an increase in corrosion resistance (Malayoglu and Neville, 2005). In this situation, chromium could be reacted with oxygen and formed chromium oxide as a protective layer at the surface of specimens. Therefore, adding more content of tungsten or molybdenum also could be proper to react with the nitrogen. Since these two elements had a higher tendency to react with nitrogen compared to chromium. It was notable that chromium provided corrosion resistance to Stellites while carbides added strength and hardness (Ma et al., 2018; Kiehl et al., 2022). However, due to the shape of polarization plots, this layer was not formed continuously on the surface of specimens. It was found that to observe a passive region for Stellite 6 a potential range of −100 to +800 mV was needed (Malayoglu and Neville, 2005). Therefore, to observe the passive behavior more potential in the anodic region should be applied (Smolina and Kobiela, 2021; Ratia et al., 2019).

The corrosion resistance of nitrided coatings was compared to the impedance of Stellite 6 and 12 coatings through the EIS method. Fig. 10 displays Nyquist and phase angle plots for all specimens. Nyquist plots showed that all specimens exhibited a similar corrosion behavior since the shape of the plots was the same. They included one semi-circular. The highest and lowest impedance were related to specimens S12 and NS6, respectively. This event demonstrated that the PN process was not an effective parameter in enhancing the corrosion resistance of nitrided specimens.

The phase angle plots showed the effect of frequency in EIS measurements. The lowest values of phase angle for specimens S6 and S12 have happened in the frequency range of 10–100 Hz. However, the lowest values for specimens NS6 and NS12 were in the frequency range of 1–30 Hz. This event could demonstrate that for nitrided specimens the diffusion layer of nitrogen was a conclusive parameter to affect the corrosion reactions. Consequently, the lowest value of phase angle was related to specimen S12. This could indicate the higher corrosion resistance for specimen S12.

For better discussion, Z-View software was utilized to suggest an electrical circuit for fitting EIS data, as shown in Fig. 10(c). In addition, the related extracted data is reported in Table 6. The circuit included three elements; the resistance of solution (
R
s
), the resistance of polarization (
R
p
), and constant phase elements of the double layer (
CPE
dl
). The change in values of
R
s
was insignificant for all specimens. The range of
R
s
values for all specimens was about 14–31 k Ω cm2. A similar
R
s
value for a 3.5% wt NaCl solution was also reported in other research (Azadi et al., 2022). The highest value of
R
p
was attributed to specimen S12 with a value of 8572 k Ω cm2. In addition, this specimen showed the lowest value of
CPE
dl
. This could show that the thickness of the double layer increased or the exposed surface to the corrosive solution was reduced. Furthermore, it was found that the water adsorption by the surface would be decreased when the capacitance of the double layer increased (Bidi et al., 2021). It was notable that n was a criterion for showing the ideal behavior of capacitance for a double layer. The lower value of n indicated a higher surface roughness. It was reported that the increase in surface roughness could be attributed to high corrosion attacks (Azadi et al., 2020a; Zhang, 2012). In this situation, the nitrided surface had lower values of n compared to the non-nitrided surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A suggested mechanism for corrosion reactions
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 11 shows a suggested mechanism of corrosion reactions for various specimens. Fig. 11(a) displays a scheme for the corrosion behavior of non-nitrided specimens. In this manner, chromium could form the oxide layer at the surface of coatings. However, this layer was not continuous, and it contained some pores. These pores were paths to penetration of corrosive species. Specimen S6 exhibited higher corrosion resistance compared to specimen S12 since it contained lower content of carbon. In other words, specimen S12 exhibited a higher corrosion rate, and this was due to the formation of Cr23C6 carbide that resulted in consuming more chromium content. It was reported that the element of chromium showed good wear and corrosion resistance. However, the formation of the oxides layer as a protective film was slow in some environments like in a 3.5% wt NaCl solution (Zhu et al., 2017).

Fig. 11(b) suggests a schematic for the corrosion mechanism of nitrided specimens. Since the PN process caused to form nitrided precipitates such as the CrN phase, the thickness of the chromium oxide layer would be decreased or more pores would be formed. Thus, nitrided specimens showed lower corrosion resistance compared to non-nitrided specimens. Furthermore, these nitrided precipitates were hard ceramic particles that could result in cracks around themselves. In this manner, corrosive ions would be diffused toward the metal through these cracks. Moreover, micro-galvanic corrosion could have occurred for nitrided coatings. When the corrosion potential for ceramic precipitates and metallic matrix was different from each other. It was notable that specimen S12 had higher corrosion resistance in comparison with specimen S6. Since Stellite 12 contained more W content to form nitrided and carbide phases. In this situation, lower content of chromium would be consumed, and the chromium oxide layer would be formed with lower defects.

In this paper, the effect of the plasma nitriding process on the wear and corrosion properties of cladded Stellite alloy on the stainless steel 410 has been investigated. The obtained results showed that the PN process was an effective process for enhancing the hardness and wear properties of Stellite 6 and 12 up to 98%. This increase was due to the formation of hard nitrided phases such as CrN, CoN, and W2N precipitates. Moreover, the PN process caused changes in the wear mechanism from micro-cutting to micro-plow and fracture mode. However, the corrosion resistance of nitrided Stellite 6 and 12 would not increase in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Since the formation of the CrN phase was an obstacle to forming the chromium oxide as a protective layer at the surface of nitrided coatings. Consequently, the highest wear property was attributed to the nitrided Stellite 12, and the highest corrosion resistance was related to the non-nitrided Stellite 6. However, adding other elements with a higher tendency to form nitrided phases compared to the CrN phase could be a suggestion to increase the corrosion resistance of nitrided Stellites.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
